title: Follow the trail
prompt: A symlink is a signpost: a name that points at another path. ls -l shows links as name -> target; readlink NAME prints just the target. 'shortcut' in the lab root is the start of a chain of symlinks — follow the signs hop by hop to the real file at the end. It holds the flag.

details.restate: shortcut points to a link, which points to another link, which points to the real (hidden) file. Follow the chain to the end and read what's there. cat shortcut also works — the shell follows links for you — but know WHERE you landed.

io.because: ls -l shows exactly where a link points

io.returns: (the FINAL real path, all hops resolved)
io.because: -f follows the whole chain in one go

terms.meaning: a file that is just a pointer to another path — deleting the link never touches the target

help.plain: Each link names the next stop. Read the signs until one isn't a sign.
help.analogy: A scavenger hunt where every clue is the address of the next clue.
help.mnemonic: ls -l shows the arrow; readlink -f rides it to the end.
help.nudge: What does ls -l print after a symlink's name? And which readlink flag resolves the WHOLE chain?
